exemplar.title: Determining Satellite Orientation with IMU Data through vector manipulations

detailedExemplarData.title: Determining Satellite Orientation with IMU Data through vector manipulations

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear macroâstructure with logical progression through introduction, theory, calculations, and evaluation
Headings and figure references aid navigation
weaknesses: Typographical errors and inconsistent formatting interrupt professional polish
Some unnecessary detail (lengthy proofs, repeated definitions) reduces conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the report is well organized and coherent (band 3), but lapses in conciseness and polish (typos, page-markers, inconsistent bibliography) justify a score of 2 rather than 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of vector/matrix notation for main steps
Effective combination of algebraic derivations, diagrams, GeoGebra images
weaknesses: Inconsistent use of symbols (e.g. â*â vs âÃâ, âI Ã· Aâ for inverse)
Algebraic slips and some unclear explanations interrupt clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and multiple representations are employed, but not consistently or errorâfree, and logical clarity is sometimes hampered. This aligns with band 2 performance.

strengths: Investigation clearly connected to personal rocket project and space-camp context
Use of bespoke GeoGebra visualisations shows initiative
weaknesses: Approach relies on standard tutorials for many steps
Creativity and independent thinking are present but not outstanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of personal engagement and initiative without reaching the highest level of innovation, consistent with band 2.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on sensor limitations, gimbal lock, and error quantification
Thoughtful analysis of results and clear evaluation of 2Â° error implications
weaknesses: Analysis remains descriptive, without probing deeper assumptions or broader implications
No sensitivity analysis or futureâoriented discussion of improvements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is meaningful and thoughtfully applied throughout, but lacks the depth and critical insight of the top band, fitting band 2.

strengths: Relevant vector and matrix methods applied effectively to the problem
Mathematics is at course level and generally correct in context
weaknesses: Minor notational errors (e.g. sign in cross-product, matrix-inverse notation)
Does not extend beyond syllabus into more advanced techniques
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Use of mathematics is appropriate and mostly accurate, demonstrating solid understanding (band 3). Occasional errors and lack of higher-level extension prevent a higher score.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations often follow a sensible progression, yet some derivations contain algebraic slips and the narrative sometimes digresses (e.g. the âdivide identity matrix by Aâ statement).  These issues interrupt clarity, so the logical communication is only âsomeâ, corresponding to band 2.

Derivations of cross products and the rotation matrix are mostly correct, but the dot-product proof contains notational errors, and the explanation of matrix inversion (âI Ã· Aâ) is inaccurate.  These are minor rather than pervasive, indicating good understanding with some errors â band 3.

The student employs equations, vector diagrams, 3-D GeoGebra images and a KSP screenshot to illustrate concepts; the multiple forms complement each other and aid understanding of the algorithm.  Although the screenshot adds limited mathematical value, the set of representations is still effective overall, meriting band 3.

The investigation moves logically from an introduction, through background theory, data processing, calibration, to an evaluation.  Each section builds on the previous one, so the reader can follow how the orientation algorithm is constructed and tested.  A few jumps in reasoning (e.g. the shift from theory to numerical comparison without detailing intermediate steps) and some typographical errors reduce the overall polish, but the narrative remains coherent and largely logical, warranting band 3 rather than 4.

Designing bespoke GeoGebra visualisations and creating a synthetic calibration example indicate a good level of initiative and some creativity, but the mathematics itself remains fairly standard.  Hence the work aligns with band 2 rather than 3.

The discussion of error sources, sensor-fusion improvements, and the role of each sensor shows thoughtful analysis, yet it remains descriptive and does not probe assumptions or alternative models in depth.  This matches the âthoughtfulâ descriptor of band 2.

The student evaluates the calculated orientation against a Kalman-derived ârealâ matrix and states implications of the 2Â° error.  While clear, the evaluation is not comprehensive (e.g., sensitivity analysis or broader applicability), so band 2 is appropriate.

The student reflects on sensor limitations, gimbal lock, and magnetic declination, and quantifies the 2Â° error, demonstrating meaningful but not deeply critical reflection.  Commentary is integrated throughout rather than confined to a short conclusion, supporting placement in band 2.

Vector and matrix notation is generally correct, but there are lapses: â*â and âÃâ are interchanged, subscripts switch style, and the inverse matrix is described as âI Ã· Aâ.  Such inconsistencies show that appropriate language is used only some of the time, not mostly consistently, placing the work in band 2.

The narrative is clearly framed around the studentâs personal experience at a space camp, and the investigation is structured in a way that reflects the studentâs own process.  This gives a recognisable personal approach, though it is not highly innovative, fitting band 2.

Most of the content is relevant to determining orientation, yet the proof of the cross-product, the lengthy calibration example with arbitrary numbers, and repeated definitions add bulk without advancing the investigation.  At 20 pages the writing is not particularly concise, so the strand fits band 2 (mostly relevant with some unnecessary elements).

Vectors, cross products, normalisation, rotation matrices and basic matrix inversion are all directly relevant to solving the stated problem, and they are applied correctly in most cases, demonstrating effectiveness rather than mere appropriateness.  Thus the strand fits band 4.

Selecting data from the studentâs own rocket project and choosing to validate a hand-built algorithm against Kalman-filter output shows noticeable individual thinking beyond textbook examples.  The mathematical steps, however, largely mirror standard tutorials, so evidence is significant but not outstanding (band 2).

Headings (Introduction, Background theory, Calculations, Evaluation, Bibliography) give a clear macro-structure, and within sections the subsections flow in a sensible order.  Figures are referenced in the text.  However, page-length markers break the flow, and there are occasional misplaced bullet characters.  Overall, the report is well-organised with a recognisable introduction, body and conclusion â comfortably within band 3.

The mathematics is firmly at AA HL level (vector and matrix operations in 3-D space) and used in context, though it does not extend beyond the syllabus into higher-level linear algebra.  That places it fully commensurate with course level, band 4.

annotations.comment: The page count statement is administrative and should be removed from the body to maintain conciseness and relevance.
In the reference-frames section, subscript âalohalâ appears to be a typo. Consistent and correct labels (e.g., \(x_{global}\)) are essential for clarity.
The dual-frame explanation links conceptually to your algorithm. To deepen reflection, discuss any challenges encountered when aligning these frames in practice.
The rotational matrix description accurately identifies its role. Clarify whether your convention uses column-vectors or row-vectors to avoid ambiguity in R construction.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes the LRF and gravity vector. However, the caption contains a typo (âAcclerationâ) and would benefit from more precise labeling of axes.
The Kerbal Space Program screenshot (Figure 2) offers an engaging representation. Consider explaining limitations of this simulation relative to real IMU data.
The title succinctly reflects the investigation focus and uses appropriate technical terms. Consider adding author details or student ID to meet academic conventions.
Your personal context from space camp provides strong engagement. To deepen this, briefly reflect on how your observations influenced specific methodological choices.
The aim statement has multiple typos (âcalcuteâ, âorentationâ) and run-on phrasing. Revise for precision and correct spelling to improve clarity.
The definition of the cross product is clear and directly tied to its application in forming a coordinate system, strengthening your mathematical communication.
The bullet ââ¢ Building Directions:â is disconnected from the heading hierarchy. Use consistent subheadings or numbered steps to improve organization.
Equations 3â4 interchange â*â and âÃâ for dot and cross products, which may confuse readers. Adopt consistent notation (e.g., use âÂ·â for dot and âÃâ for cross).
Expanding the dot-product proof is correctly performed, but adding a brief sentence explaining why term cancellations occur would enhance clarity.
The Q.E.D section concludes the proof neatly. Consider linking this orthogonality result back to how it informs your orientation matrix construction.
The explanation of normalization is concise. Including a numeric example applying \(\hat{\vec v}=\vec v/|\vec v|\) to one of your measured vectors would illustrate the process.
Figure 3 is clear but the caption misspells âmagneticâ as âmangeticâ. Proofreading captions prevents distraction and supports professional presentation.
Your calculations are well structured with clear steps. To improve mathematical accuracy, state the rounding convention explicitly when you report numerical results.
Normalization of the magnetometer vector is computed correctly. For completeness, you could show the intermediate decimal values before rounding to three significant figures.
The cross-product determinants in Steps 2 and 3 follow logically. However, double-check the sign of the j-component in Step 2 to ensure consistency with your numeric result.
Constructing R by placing unit vectors as columns is correct. Mention that R transforms LRFâGRF explicitly to align with standard convention.
Defining the error matrix and using trace to compute the angle is methodologically sound. Cite the formulaâs origin directly in the text to strengthen academic rigor.
You correctly apply the error-angle formula to find ~2.00Â°. To deepen reflection, discuss potential impacts of this error on mission objectives.
The calibration model is introduced clearly with equation \(B_{measured}=AB_{ideal}+b\). To improve, explicitly define matrix dimensions and any symmetry assumptions.
Your practical calibration example is instructive. However, performing a sensitivity analysis on parameter choices A and b could demonstrate robustness of the method.
The inverse-matrix calculation uses âI Ã· A,â which is nonstandard. Show the formal algebraic steps (i.e., compute \(A^{-1}\) via reciprocals for diagonal matrices) to support understanding.
The final evaluation discusses gimbal lock and sensor fusion well. To link back to your work, suggest how quaternions could integrate with your vector method.
Bibliography entries show variety of sources, but formatting is inconsistent (e.g., date placement, capitalization). Apply a consistent citation style for presentation polish.
